description: The megamouth shark is one of the rarest and most mysterious of all shark species, with fewer than 300 recorded observations since it was first discovered in 1976 when one became entangled in a US Navy anchor off Hawaii. It is the third known filter-feeding shark after the whale shark and basking shark, using its enormous mouth to engulf clouds of shrimp, jellyfish, and copepods. Its luminescent tissue inside its mouth may act as a lure to attract prey in dark water.
funFact: The megamouth shark was entirely unknown to science until 1976 — a reminder that the deep ocean still harbors large, previously unknown animals. Despite being over 5 m long, it eluded discovery for all of recorded human history before that first accidental capture.
habitat: Open ocean in tropical to temperate latitudes, performing daily vertical migrations — spending daylight hours at depths of 120–166 m and rising to within 12–25 m of the surface at night to follow migrating layers of planktonic prey. Found across the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans.
geographicRangeDescription: Rarely encountered; fewer than 300 confirmed sightings since its discovery in 1976. Records span the Pacific (Japan, California, the Philippines, and Australia), the Atlantic (Brazil, West Africa), and the Indian Ocean (South Africa, Indonesia). Japan accounts for the most documented encounters. Its true global abundance is unknown, and what appears to be rarity may partly reflect the difficulty of detecting a deep-water, filter-feeding species.
diet: Almost exclusively small planktonic prey — primarily euphausiids (krill), copepods, and jellyfish — engulfed in vast quantities by ram filter feeding. Its enormous, wide mouth and fine gill rakers strain prey from water as it swims slowly forward. Bioluminescent tissue lining the upper jaw may help lure prey into the open mouth in dimly lit water.
size: One of the largest filter-feeding sharks: females reach ~5.5 m (18 ft) and an estimated ~1,215 kg (2,679 lbs); males are smaller at ~4 m (13 ft). Robust and broadly built compared to other lamniform sharks, with a distinctive oversized, flabby head.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — fewer than 300 recorded individuals since 1976

vitalSigns.label: Lifespan
vitalSigns.value: Unknown; likely several decades based on growth rate inference

vitalSigns.label: Max Length
vitalSigns.value: ~5.5 m (18 ft) females; ~4 m (13 ft) males

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: Unknown; likely viviparous with small litter sizes; no confirmed nursery areas observed

vitalSigns.label: Top Speed
vitalSigns.value: Slow — a passive ram filter feeder with little capacity for sustained pursuit

vitalSigns.label: Ecological Role
vitalSigns.value: Mesopelagic filter feeder; links deep plankton layers to larger predators

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Euphausiids (krill), copepods, jellyfish, other small planktonic invertebrates

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Unknown; presumed low due to large body size and likely slow maturation

vitalSigns.label: Key Senses
vitalSigns.value: Possible bioluminescent lure in jaw; lateral line; ampullae of Lorenzini for electroreception

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical to temperate latitudes in the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans; records from Japan, Hawaii, California, Australia, Philippines, Brazil, South Africa, and Indonesia

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable population estimates exist. Fewer than 300 individuals have ever been recorded, making any trend analysis nearly impossible. The values shown are illustrative of presumed slow, uncertain pressure from increasing bycatch and deep-sea fishing activity. The species' rarity and deep-water habits make direct population assessment effectively impossible with current methods.

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Krill and mesopelagic fish stocks under growing pressure from expanding midwater trawl fisheries

preyDeclineRegions.name: W. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Dense euphausiid aggregations targeted by commercial krill fisheries in some areas; jellyfish blooms are shifting

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indian Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Deep planktonic prey stocks relatively intact; limited targeted midwater fishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: Tropical Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Core habitat largely unfished at depth; prey availability considered stable

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Open ocean deep waters largely undisturbed; prey availability stable

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Occasionally caught as bycatch in deep-set longlines, driftnets, and midwater trawls; Japan accounts for the majority of recorded captures

fishingPressureRegions.name: W. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Multiple bycatch records from Taiwan, Philippines, and Indonesia in driftnet and longline fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indian Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Rare bycatch incidents reported; limited deep-sea fishing overlap

fishingPressureRegions.name: Tropical Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Sporadic incidental captures; most observations are strandings rather than fishing encounters

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Very few records; minimal fishing pressure at relevant depths in known range

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Unknown — presumed stable to slowly declining with increasing bycatch exposure

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Generally intact — open-ocean mesopelagic zone largely undisturbed

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Likely stable; some krill and jellyfish dynamics shifting with climate

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Low but increasing — incidental bycatch across Pacific and Indian Oceans

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Minimal — no targeted protections; benefits from depth and open-ocean distribution

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: The megamouth shark's vertical migration into shallower surface waters at night brings it into overlap with driftnets, longlines, and pelagic trawls. Japan, Taiwan, and the Philippines record the most captures. As fishing pressure in the Pacific intensifies, encounter rates are expected to rise.

threats.name: Data Deficiency
threats.description: With fewer than 300 known individuals on record, almost nothing is known about population size, age structure, or reproductive rates. Decline could go undetected for decades. The Least Concern designation reflects ignorance as much as safety.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming seas and ocean deoxygenation may compress the depth range of suitable mesopelagic habitat and shift the distribution of planktonic prey. Changes to diel vertical migration patterns in prey could disrupt the megamouth's feeding strategy.

threats.name: Plastic Pollution
threats.description: Filter feeders are particularly vulnerable to ingesting microplastics concentrated in surface waters at night. Though no confirmed cases exist for this species, its feeding mechanism and surface-water presence make plastic ingestion a plausible risk.

threats.name: Driftnet Fisheries
threats.description: Large-mesh driftnets — banned on the high seas but still used in some national waters and illegally at sea — pose a direct entanglement risk to a slow, large-bodied shark that surfaces predictably each night.

actionItems.title: Support High Seas Treaty Ratification
actionItems.description: The UN High Seas Treaty would establish marine protected areas in international waters covering significant portions of the megamouth's open-ocean range. Urge your government to ratify and implement the treaty.

actionItems.title: Push for Driftnet Bans and Enforcement
actionItems.description: Advocate for stronger enforcement of the UN moratorium on high-seas driftnets and support national bans on driftnet use in exclusive economic zones where megamouth sharks are known to occur.

actionItems.title: Fund Megamouth Research
actionItems.description: Support institutions using satellite tagging, ROV surveys, and environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling to learn basic biology — population size, movement, reproduction — that remains almost entirely unknown for this species.

actionItems.title: Demand Bycatch Reporting
actionItems.description: Urge regional fisheries management organizations (RFMOs) in the Pacific and Indian Oceans to mandate reporting of all elasmobranch bycatch, including megamouth sharks. Without data, meaningful protection is impossible.

actionItems.title: Reduce Plastic Waste
actionItems.description: Filter feeders like the megamouth shark are directly exposed to microplastic pollution during their nightly surface feeding. Reducing single-use plastics and supporting ocean cleanup initiatives directly benefits this and other filter-feeding species.
